skill.name: minimax-xlsx
skill.description: Create, inspect, edit, format, and validate Excel and spreadsheet files using OOXML-based helper scripts.
skill.summary: Work with XLSX, XLSM, CSV, and TSV files while preserving workbook structure and formulas.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many alerts, but most network, C2, weak-crypto, and shell-pattern hits are false positives from Markdown examples, OOXML namespace URLs, and spreadsheet terminology. The remaining real risks are legitimate but important: helper scripts process user-controlled workbook archives, recursively replace a caller-supplied work directory, and can run LibreOffice headlessly on untrusted files.

medium_findings.title: Headless LibreOffice Execution on User Workbooks
medium_findings.description: The recalculation helper launches LibreOffice with subprocess.run on a user-provided spreadsheet copy. Arguments are passed as a list without shell=True, so command injection is not evident, but opening untrusted Office files in a full office engine can expose the host to parser vulnerabilities, external content behavior, hangs, or resource exhaustion.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call and documentation confirm that LibreOffice opens and recalculates user-controlled spreadsheets. The risk is contextual rather than command injection because the command uses argv lists and a timeout.

medium_findings.title: Caller-Controlled Directory Removal
medium_findings.description: The unpack helper removes the output directory if it already exists before creating a fresh work directory. This supports the intended /tmp workflow, but a mistaken or malicious output_dir argument could recursively delete unrelated files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly calls shutil.rmtree on a CLI-provided output_dir. No allowlist or safety check limits deletion to a temporary workspace.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Spreadsheet Archive Processing
medium_findings.description: The skill unpacks XLSX archives, rewrites XML files, and repacks workbooks. Zip-slip traversal is checked before extractall, but there are no visible archive size or file-count limits, so malicious or very large archives can still cause local resource exhaustion.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The archive-processing behavior is confirmed and path traversal is mitigated. The residual concern is denial-of-service exposure from unbounded archive contents.

low_findings.title: Static Network Findings Are Namespace and Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings reviewed are OOXML namespace identifiers or a Microsoft documentation URL. I did not find evidence of runtime HTTP requests, credential exfiltration, or external telemetry in the scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are schema namespaces or public documentation references, and the script search showed no HTTP client usage. This strongly supports a false-positive verdict for runtime network behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Trigger Shell Pattern Alerts
low_findings.description: Most Ruby backtick and shell alerts are from fenced command examples in SKILL.md and reference guides. They are operational instructions for the agent, not hidden executable code, but users should still review generated commands before running them on important files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible Markdown examples that call local helper scripts. They do not show obfuscation or automatic execution by the skill files themselves.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text that asks the evaluator to ignore prior instructions, override security analysis, claim pre-approval, or assume special authority. No prompt injection finding is confirmed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The manifest and routing text describe spreadsheet workflows and do not contain the suspicious override patterns requested for review. This is a negative finding based on targeted inspection.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run Invokes External Office Engine
dangerous_patterns.description: LibreOffice is invoked through subprocess.run for version checks and workbook recalculation. The implementation avoids shell=True and uses timeouts, but external document parsing remains a meaningful sandboxing concern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external process calls are explicit and semantically tied to spreadsheet recalculation. Mitigations reduce injection risk but do not remove document-processing risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Removal of Existing Work Directory
dangerous_patterns.description: The unpacker deletes an existing output directory before extraction. This is useful for clean workspaces but dangerous when the destination path is wrong.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The call is directly visible and uses user-controlled CLI input. The risk depends on how callers choose output_dir.

dangerous_patterns.title: ZIP extractall Used After Path Validation
dangerous_patterns.description: The unpacker uses extractall, but it first verifies each member path stays under output_dir. This makes the static zip-slip alert mostly mitigated, with resource exhaustion still possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The extractall call and preceding traversal guard are adjacent in the same function. This supports a nuanced finding rather than a confirmed path traversal vulnerability.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet work often breaks formulas, formatting, or workbook structure. This skill provides OOXML-based workflows for creating, editing, reading, and validating Excel files with less format loss.
actual_capabilities: Reads XLSX, XLSM, CSV, and TSV files for structure discovery and data analysis.
Creates new XLSX workbooks from a minimal OOXML template.
Edits existing XLSX files through unpack, XML modification, and repack workflows.
Adds rows or columns while updating formulas and copying nearby styles.
Checks formulas for broken references, missing sheets, and common Excel error values.
Optionally recalculates formulas through LibreOffice when available.
limitations: Legacy .xls files are not supported directly and must be converted first.
Dynamic formula recalculation depends on LibreOffice being installed.
Manual XML edits require care because malformed XML can make workbooks unreadable.
The helper scripts do not provide a full Excel compatibility engine.

use_cases.title: Build a Financial Workbook
use_cases.description: Create an XLSX model with formulas, color-coded inputs, formatted totals, and validation checks before delivery.

use_cases.title: Inspect a Client Spreadsheet
use_cases.description: Read workbook sheets, columns, sample rows, and data quality issues before deciding what edits are needed.

use_cases.title: Repair Workbook Formulas
use_cases.description: Unpack a workbook, fix broken formulas or references, repack the file, and run formula checks.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize a Spreadsheet
prompt_templates.prompt: Read this spreadsheet and summarize the sheets, columns, row counts, and any data quality issues you find.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Excel workbook from this table. Add formulas for totals, apply professional financial formatting, and validate the output file.

prompt_templates.title: Edit Without Format Loss
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the existing workbook by adding the requested row and formulas. Preserve all sheets, styles, formulas, and workbook structure.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Recalculate
prompt_templates.prompt: Run static formula validation on this workbook. If LibreOffice is available, recalculate formulas and report any remaining formula errors.

output_examples.input: A user provides a CSV file and asks for an Excel workbook with totals and formatted headers.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates an XLSX file, adds formula-based totals, applies readable financial formatting, and confirms validation results.

output_examples.input: A user uploads an existing workbook and asks to insert a new expense row.
output_examples.output: The assistant edits the workbook structure, shifts affected rows, updates formulas, preserves original sheets, and verifies the output.

output_examples.input: A user asks whether a financial model has broken formulas.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports broken sheet references, Excel error values, and whether dynamic recalculation was completed or skipped.

best_practices: Work on a copy of important spreadsheets and keep the original file unchanged.
Use temporary work directories when unpacking XLSX files for XML edits.
Run formula validation after every create, edit, or repair workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not use openpyxl round trips for complex existing workbooks with macros or pivot tables.
Do not hardcode calculated values when an Excel formula is required.
Do not unpack a workbook into an important existing directory.

faq.question: Can this skill edit existing Excel files?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses an unpack, XML edit, and repack workflow designed to preserve workbook structure.

faq.question: Does it support CSV files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can read CSV and TSV files and use them as input for spreadsheet analysis or workbook creation.

faq.question: Can it recalculate formulas?
faq.answer: It can use LibreOffice for dynamic recalculation when LibreOffice is installed in the environment.

faq.question: Does it preserve macros?
faq.answer: The workflow avoids normal openpyxl round trips, which helps preserve workbook parts, but macro safety still needs care.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on untrusted workbooks?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill processes archive contents and may open files with LibreOffice for recalculation.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
